perturbation.original_text: All right title and interest in and to the Confidential Information shall remain with Disclosing Par ty or its licensors. Nothing in this Agreement is intended to grant any rights to Recipient under any patents, copyrights, trademarks, or trade secrets of Disclosing Party. ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PROVIDED "AS IS". THE DISCLOSING PARTY MAKES NO WAR RANTIES, EXPRESS, IMPLIED OR OTHERWISE, REGARDING NON -INFRINGEMENT OF THIRD PARTY RIGHTS OR ITS ACCURACY, COMPLETENESS OR PERFORMANCE.
perturbation.changed_text: All right title and interest in and to the Confidential Information shall remain with Disclosing Party or its licensors. Nothing in this Agreement is intended to grant any rights to Recipient under any patents, copyrights, trademarks, or trade secrets of Disclosing Party. ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PROVIDED "AS IS". THE DISCLOSING PARTY MAKES NO WARRANTIES, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the disclaimer regarding non-infringement of third-party rights and accuracy/completeness/performance of the confidential information could create liability issues. While NDAs often disclaim warranties, removing specific disclaimers about third-party rights may conflict with implied obligations to not provide information that infringes on others' intellectual property. This particularly problematic if the confidential information is later found to infringe on a third party's copyright after usage by the recipient of the information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-312. Warranty of Title and Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the disclaimer of warranties regarding non-infringement, the agreement arguably opens the door for a claim that the Disclosing Party implicitly warrants that the information provided does not infringe on third-party rights. UCC 2-312 implies a warranty of title and against infringement in sales of goods, and while this isn't a direct sale of goods, providing information could be construed similarly enough to raise concerns.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-312. Warranty of Title and Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-312. Warranty of Title and Against Infringement; Buyer's Obligation Against Infringement. (1) Subject to subsection (2) there is in a contract for sale a warranty by the seller that (a) the title conveyed shall be good, and its transfer rightful; and (b) the goods shall be delivered free from any security interest or other lien or encumbrance of which the buyer at the time of contracting has no knowledge. (2) A warranty under subsection (1) will be excluded or modified only by specific language or by circumstances which give the buyer reason to know that the person selling does not claim title in himself or that he is purporting to sell only such right or title as he or a third person may have. (3) Unless otherwise agreed a seller who is a merchant regularly dealing in goods of the kind warrants that the goods shall be delivered free of the rightful claim of any third person by way of infringement or the like but a buyer who furnishes specifications to the seller must hold the seller harmless against any such claim which arises out of compliance with the specifications. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The omission of the disclaimer regarding non-infringement of third-party rights in the modified contract text creates a potential legal contradiction. UCC 2-312, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, implies a warranty against infringement in the sale of goods. Although an NDA is not strictly a sale of goods, the Law Explanation suggests a potential argument that providing information could be construed similarly. The removal of the disclaimer arguably opens the door for a claim that the Disclosing Party implicitly warrants that the information provided does not infringe on third-party rights. By removing this protection, the modified contract increases the Disclosing Party's potential liability. This contradiction falls under the definition of 'Omission Legal Contradiction' because a legally important disclaimer was removed.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Recipient shall breach this Agreement, or in the event that a breach appears to be imminent, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to all legal and equitable remedies afforded it by law, and in addition may recover all reaso nable costs and attorneys' fees incurred in seeking such remedies. If the Confidential Information is sought by any third party, including by way of subpoena or other court process, the Recipient shall inform the Disclosing Party of the request in suffici ent time to permit the Disclosing Party to object to and, if necessary, seek court intervention to prevent the disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Recipient shall breach this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to legal remedies. If the Confidential Information is sought by any third party, the Recipient shall inform the Disclosing Party of the request.
perturbation.explanation: By removing "equitable remedies afforded it by law" and "reasonable costs and attorneys' fees" along with removing "in sufficient time to permit the Disclosing Party to object to and, if necessary, seek court intervention to prevent the disclosure," the contract limits the Disclosing Party's recourse in case of a breach. In Colorado, the law typically allows for both legal and equitable remedies, as well as the potential for attorney's fees in breach of contract cases. The removed text related to the time for the Disclosing party to respond is also very important.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado Revised Statutes § 13-17-101 et seq. (Colorado Uniform Trade Secrets Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Colorado Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) allows for injunctive relief (an equitable remedy) and, in some cases, attorney's fees for misappropriation of trade secrets. By limiting remedies to only 'legal remedies' and omitting the specific provision for attorney's fees, the contract could be interpreted as waiving rights available under CUTSA, especially concerning confidential information that qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the Disclosing Party's entitlement to 'equitable remedies afforded it by law' and 'reasonable costs and attorneys' fees.' Additionally, it removes the requirement for the Recipient to inform the Disclosing Party 'in sufficient time to permit the Disclosing Party to object to and, if necessary, seek court intervention to prevent the disclosure.' This omission directly contradicts the principle that parties are typically entitled to both legal and equitable remedies, as well as attorney's fees, in breach of contract cases, and sufficient response time to address potential issues. The Law Explanation further emphasizes this point, stating that the Colorado Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) allows for injunctive relief (an equitable remedy) and, in some cases, attorney's fees for misappropriation of trade secrets. The omissions of these remedies weakens the legal protections afforded to the disclosing party and creates a loophole that would likely fail legal scrutiny, thus leading to a legal contradiction. While the scraped snippets are unavailable, the explanations make it clear that this weakens the legal protections afforded to the disclosing party.

perturbation.original_text: The validity, construction and enforceability of this Agreement shall be governed in all respects by the law of the state of Colorado . This Agreement may not be amended except in writing signed by a duly authorized representative of the respective Parties. This Agreement shall control in the event of a conflict with any other agreement between the Parties with respect to the sub ject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity, construction and enforceability of this Agreement shall be governed by the law of the state of Colorado. This Agreement may be amended. This Agreement shall control in the event of a conflict with any other agreement between the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement that amendments must be in writing and signed can create enforceability issues. Colorado law, like that of most states, respects the parties' agreement on how to amend a contract. If a contract requires written amendments, oral modifications may not be enforceable. By deleting the 'in writing signed by a duly authorized representative' part, that consumer protection measure is gone.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado law regarding contract modification (common law principles)
perturbation.law_citation: Colorado common law on contract modification
perturbation.law_explanation: While there's no specific statute, Colorado common law generally enforces clauses requiring written modifications. Deleting this requirement means oral agreements might be argued as valid amendments, potentially leading to disputes and uncertainty about the terms of the agreement.  The case law confirms that "A written contract is deemed to express the entire agreement of the parties and evidence of a previous or contemporaneous oral agreement is generally inadmissible to vary, contradict, or add to the terms of the written contract." (though that is not the exact issue here, it provides a precedent).

You must accept these Terms & Conditions to use the service. Please also review the Privacy & Cookies policy
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates that amendments must be in writing and signed. The changed text removes this requirement, allowing for oral amendments. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is empty, and Snippet 2 leads to a LexisNexis login page and doesn't provide direct legal text, the Law Explanation states that Colorado common law generally enforces clauses requiring written modifications. Removing this requirement creates a potential contradiction because oral agreements might then be argued as valid amendments, leading to disputes. The Perturbation Explanation reinforces that this deletion removes a consumer protection measure, creating enforceability issues. The removal of the 'in writing signed by a duly authorized representative' portion directly contradicts the principle of enforcing written modification clauses, thus, there is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Pitch Deck F ire confirms that this information has been made cl ear and publically available to all event attendees. Pitch Deck Fire agrees that if a Participant delivers to Pitch Deck Fire any information or data marked or identified as confidential or proprietary ("confidential information"), then Pitch Deck Fire shall not, except as otherwise permitted or instructed by the applicable Participant in writing (a) disclose or otherwise make available the confidential information to any third party except to the extent otherwise expressly permitted by this Agreement, (b) modify, copy, transmit, alter, merge, decompile, dis assemble, reverse engineer or adapt any portion of the confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Pitch Deck Fire confirms that this information has been made cl ear and publically available to all event attendees. Pitch Deck Fire agrees that if a Participant delivers to Pitch Deck Fire any information or data marked or identified as confidential or proprietary ("confidential information"), then Pitch Deck Fire shall not disclose or otherwise make available the confidential information to any third party, or modify, copy, transmit, alter, merge, decompile, dis assemble, reverse engineer or adapt any portion of the confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "except as otherwise permitted or instructed by the applicable Participant in writing" from both clauses (a) and (b), this NDA now prohibits Pitch Deck Fire from disclosing or modifying participant's confidential information *even if the participant gives explicit permission to do so*. This creates an unreasonable restriction and could be considered unconscionable under Colorado law, as it restricts the participant's control over their own confidential information, removing the possibility for the participant to allow for specific limited uses of their data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado case law on unconscionability in contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado courts have held that a contract or clause is unconscionable if it is 'improvident, unfair, or manifestly unjust.' Davis v. M.L.G. Corp., 712 P.2d 985, 989 (Colo. 1986). By prohibiting Pitch Deck Fire from ever disclosing or modifying the participant's confidential information, even with explicit written permission, the agreement becomes so one-sided that it could be deemed unconscionable and therefore unenforceable under Colorado law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Kohl v. Union Ins. Co. :: 1986 :: Colorado Supreme Court Decisions :: Colorado Case Law :: Colorado Law :: U.S. Law :: Justia Kohl v. Union Ins. Co. Annotate this Case 731 P.2d 134 (1986) Rex A. KOHL and Tony L. Martino, Petitioners, v. UNION INSURANCE COMPANY, a Nebraska corporation, Respondent. Carol Sue CONNELLY, Petitioner, v. UNION INSURANCE COMPANY, a Nebraska corporation, Respondent. Nos. 84SC381, 84SC387. Supreme Court of Colorado, En Banc. December 22, 1986. Rehearing Denied February 9, 1987. McDermott, Hansen, Anderson & Reilly, Leland P. Anderson, Denver, for petitioners Rex A. Kohl and Tony L. Martino. McDermott, Kiehnhoff & Meconi, John A. McDermott, Canon City, for petitioner Carol Sue Connelly. Hall & Evans, Richard A. Hanneman, Gordon L. Vaughan, Denver, for respondent. ERICKSON, Justice. We granted certiorari to review the court of appeals decision in Union Insurance Co. v. Connelly, 694 P.2d 354 (Colo.App.1984), which affirmed a trial court determination that the accidental discharge of a rifle did not arise from the use of the insured's vehicle for purposes of coverage *135 under an automobile insurance policy. We reverse and remand with directions. I. The facts are not in dispute. On October 12, 1980, Carrol Ray Weaver, Phyllip Connelly, Terry Clear, Rex Kohl, and Tony Martino were returning from a hunting trip in the mountains west of Canon City, Colorado. Kohl and Martino were in one vehicle, Connelly and Clear in another, and Weaver in a third. Weaver's hunting rifle was in a gun rack mounted above the dashboard of his jeep. On the road back to Canon City, the group stopped for refreshments at a store in Cotopaxi, Colorado. When Weaver arrived, the other hunters had left their vehicles and were conversing in the parking lot. Weaver parked his jeep alongside the other hunters, and joined the group. After a short conversation, Weaver returned to his jeep to retrieve a gas can. While reaching inside the jeep to get his key to the gas cap, Weaver decided to remove his rifle fr
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the participant's ability to permit Pitch Deck Fire to disclose or modify confidential information, even with explicit written permission. This directly contradicts the principle that parties should have control over their own information and contractual relationships. While the scraped snippets don't directly address this scenario, the Law Explanation states that prohibiting disclosure or modification even with permission could be deemed unconscionable under Colorado law, making the agreement so one-sided that it could be unenforceable. The removal of the 'except as otherwise permitted' clause creates an unreasonable restriction, thus a contradiction exists.
